library.title: Cross-Training & Recovery Library
library.description: A picker-style library of activities for evening training and recovery. Unlike the Minimalift lifting program (date-ordered), this is a catalog: pick any activity, log its metrics, track improvement over time.
library.use_pattern: User picks one or more activities per evening, logs metrics for each. App stores per-session log entries keyed by date and activity ID.

categories.name: Squash
categories.description: Court-based racquet sport. Solo drills build technique and footwork; match play tests it under pressure.
categories.general_progression_tip: Spend 60-70% of training time on solo drills (drives, boasts, volleys, ghosting) and 30-40% on match play. Solo drills are where stroke quality is built; matches are where you stress-test it.

activities.name: Ghosting
activities.description: Move to each of the 6 court corners (FL, FR, ML, MR, BL, BR) without a ball, simulating shot footwork. Builds movement patterns and squash-specific conditioning.
activities.improvement_tip: Start with 6 corners x 4 rounds, 60s rest. Progress by either lowering rest (60s -> 45s -> 30s) or adding rounds (4 -> 6 -> 8). Don't add both at once.

activities.name: Solo Length Drives
activities.description: Hit straight drives down the wall, alternating forehand and backhand, aiming for the back third of the court. The single most important drill for squash.
activities.improvement_tip: Aim for 50 consecutive forehand drives landing past the short line, then 50 backhand. Once consistent, narrow the target zone (back third only, then back quarter).

activities.name: Solo Boast & Drive
activities.description: Hit a boast (3-wall) from the back, sprint to the front, hit a straight drive, return. Combines movement and shot variety.
activities.improvement_tip: Start with sets of 10 boast-drive sequences. Track how many you can complete without losing the ball. Add a volley before the boast to build the full pattern.

activities.name: Solo Volley Drills
activities.description: Stand mid-court, volley the ball repeatedly off the front wall above the cut line. Builds racquet control and reaction speed.
activities.improvement_tip: Target 50 unbroken volleys, then add constraints: forehand-only, backhand-only, alternating, or above-cut-line only.

activities.name: Target Practice (Cones in Corners)
activities.description: Place targets (cones, water bottles, towels) in back corners. Hit from the T, score 1 point per target hit out of 50 shots.
activities.improvement_tip: Move the target progressively further into the corner as accuracy improves. Add a feeder shot (boast-then-drive into target) once static accuracy is solid.

activities.name: Condition Game
activities.description: Match play with restrictions (e.g., 'length only' = no boasts/drops, 'one-sided' = one player must hit only forehand). Forces specific patterns under pressure.
activities.improvement_tip: Pick one weakness (e.g., backhand drives) and use a condition game that forces it. 30 minutes of condition games beats 30 minutes of unrestricted match play for skill development.

activities.name: Match Play
activities.description: Standard match (best of 3 or 5 games to 11). The pressure test for everything.

metrics.label: Opponent level
metrics.note: Higher number = stronger opponent. Context for interpreting wins/losses.

activities.improvement_tip: Track win rate against opponents of similar level over rolling 10-match windows. Note one tactical takeaway per match — patterns reveal themselves over months.

categories.name: Running
categories.description: Aerobic training with five distinct workout types. The 80/20 rule: roughly 80% of running should be easy, 20% hard.
categories.general_progression_tip: Build aerobic base first with easy runs. Add ONE quality session per week (tempo, intervals, OR hills — not all three). Increase weekly mileage by no more than 10% week-over-week.

activities.name: Easy / Zone 2 Run
activities.description: Conversational pace, low heart rate. The bedrock of aerobic fitness — the run you should do most often.

metrics.label: Avg pace
metrics.note: At constant easy HR, faster pace over time = improvement

metrics.label: Avg HR
metrics.note: At constant easy pace, lower HR over time = improvement

activities.improvement_tip: Stay below ~75% of max HR (or 'can hold a conversation' pace). Most people run their easy days too hard. If unsure, slow down — it's the workout that compounds the most.

activities.name: Tempo / Threshold Run
activities.description: Sustained 'comfortably hard' pace, just below the point where lactate accumulates. 20-40 min at threshold pace, after a warmup.
activities.improvement_tip: Once a week max. Start with 2x10 min @ threshold w/ 2 min easy between. Progress to 1x20 min, then 1x30, then 1x40 over a few months.

activities.name: Intervals (VO2 Max)
activities.description: Short, hard repeats with recovery — e.g., 6x800m or 10x400m at 5K-race pace or faster.

metrics.label: Rest between reps
metrics.note: Same pace with shorter rest = improved recovery capacity

activities.improvement_tip: Don't make every interval session faster. Alternate weeks: pace progression (same volume, faster) and volume progression (same pace, more reps). Once a week max — these are taxing.

activities.name: Long Run
activities.description: The longest run of the week, at easy pace. Builds endurance, capillary density, and mental resilience.
activities.improvement_tip: Long run should be 25-30% of weekly mileage, no more. Build distance over months, not weeks. If targeting a race, peak long run is ~75-80% of race distance for half-marathon and below.

activities.name: Hill Repeats
activities.description: Run hard up a hill (30s-90s), jog/walk back down. Builds power, strength, and form without the impact of flat sprints.

metrics.label: Hill effort time
metrics.note: Same hill, faster time = power improvement

activities.improvement_tip: Start with 6x30s hill repeats. Add 1-2 reps every other week. Once you're at 12 reps, increase the hill duration to 60s and reset rep count to 6.

activities.name: Strides
activities.description: 4-6 x 20s of fast, relaxed running with full recovery, usually at the end of an easy run. Maintains turnover and form.
activities.improvement_tip: These aren't a workout, they're a tune-up. 2-3 sessions per week of 4-6 strides keeps your fast-twitch fibers and form sharp during base-building.

activities.name: 5K Time Trial
activities.description: Hard solo 5K. The benchmark workout — a single number that tracks fitness over months.
activities.improvement_tip: Run this every 6-8 weeks, not more. Same course, same conditions if possible. The trend matters far more than any single time.

categories.name: Core
categories.description: Anti-extension, anti-rotation, and anti-lateral-flexion work. The core's job is to resist movement, not produce it.
categories.general_progression_tip: Pick one exercise from each of the three categories (anti-extension, anti-rotation, anti-lateral) per session. Progress by adding hold time, reps, or load — but only one variable at a time.

activities.name: Plank (Anti-Extension)
activities.description: Hold a forearm plank with neutral spine. Resists lower-back extension.
activities.improvement_tip: Cap holds at 60s; beyond that, you're testing endurance, not core strength. Once you can hold 60s with perfect form, progress to RKC plank (max-tension version) or weighted plank.

activities.name: Side Plank (Anti-Lateral Flexion)
activities.description: Hold a side plank on forearm. Resists sideways spinal flexion.
activities.improvement_tip: Note any left-right asymmetry — it's common and worth correcting. If one side is 30%+ weaker, do one extra set on that side until it catches up.

activities.name: Hollow Body Hold
activities.description: Lie on back, lift legs and shoulders off floor, lower back pressed down. Builds total anterior core tension.
activities.improvement_tip: Start tucked (knees bent, arms by sides), progress to one-leg extended, then both legs extended, then both legs and arms extended overhead. Each progression adds a lever, not just time.

activities.name: Dead Bug
activities.description: Lie on back, alternate lowering opposite arm and leg while keeping lower back pressed to floor. Anti-extension with limb dissociation.

metrics.label: Tempo per rep
metrics.note: Slower = harder. 5s down, 5s up is brutal.

activities.improvement_tip: Quality over quantity. If your lower back lifts off the floor at any point, the rep doesn't count. Progress tempo before reps.

activities.name: Pallof Press (Anti-Rotation)
activities.description: Stand sideways to a cable or band, press handle straight out from chest, resist the rotational pull. The premier anti-rotation exercise.
activities.improvement_tip: Add a 2-second pause at full extension. Progress: standing -> half-kneeling -> tall-kneeling -> single-leg standing. Each stance forces more core stabilization.

activities.name: Hanging Leg Raise
activities.description: Hang from a bar, lift legs to horizontal (or higher) without swinging. Strict version is significantly harder than knee raises.
activities.improvement_tip: Progression: knee raises -> straight-leg raises to horizontal -> toes-to-bar -> windshield wipers. If swinging starts, the rep doesn't count.

activities.name: Suitcase Carry
activities.description: Carry a heavy weight in one hand, walk for distance/time without leaning. Loaded anti-lateral-flexion plus grip.
activities.improvement_tip: Goal is bodyweight in one hand for 60s per side. Walk slow and tall — if you start tilting, the weight is too heavy.

activities.name: Ab Wheel Rollout
activities.description: From knees (or feet), roll the wheel forward, extend arms overhead, return to start without sagging. Anti-extension under load.
activities.improvement_tip: Master 10 strict kneeling rollouts before attempting standing. Standing is a 5x harder progression — most people skip the kneeling phase too early.

categories.name: Plyometrics
categories.description: Explosive, ground-contact-driven exercises that build power and reactive strength. Overlap with squash and running carryover.
categories.general_progression_tip: Plyos are quality, not quantity work. Stop the set when output drops — fewer high-quality reps beats more fatigued reps. Cap total ground contacts at ~80-120 per session.

activities.name: Box Jump
activities.description: Two-foot jump onto a box, stand tall, step (don't jump) down. Pure concentric power.
activities.improvement_tip: Step DOWN, not jump down. Most box-jump injuries happen on the descent. Box height is a vanity metric beyond ~30 inches; switch focus to loaded jumps or depth jumps for real progression.

activities.name: Broad Jump
activities.description: Standing horizontal jump for distance. Tests concentric power in the horizontal plane.
activities.improvement_tip: Track max distance monthly — daily max attempts wear down the nervous system. Use submaximal jumps (90% effort) for most reps and test true max only every 4-6 weeks.

activities.name: Depth Jump
activities.description: Step off a box, land, and immediately rebound jump up. Trains reactive strength and short ground contact times.

metrics.label: Ground contact time
metrics.note: Sub-200ms is the goal. Phone slow-mo + frame counting gets you close enough.

activities.improvement_tip: Start with 12-18 inch drop. The goal is to MINIMIZE ground contact time, not maximize rebound. If contact time goes above 250ms, the box is too high — drop it.

activities.name: Pogo Jumps
activities.description: Quick, low, two-foot rebound jumps with stiff ankles and minimal knee bend. Builds Achilles and calf reactive strength.
activities.improvement_tip: Stay tall — the bounce comes from the ankles, not the hips. Aim for 50+ reps in 30 seconds before adding load or moving to single-leg variants.

activities.name: Lateral Bound
activities.description: Single-leg jump sideways, land on the opposite leg, stick the landing or rebound. Trains lateral power for squash and change-of-direction.

metrics.label: Stuck landings %
metrics.note: A stuck landing = no extra hop or stumble

activities.improvement_tip: Stick every landing for the first month. Once you can stick 10/10, start adding a rebound (continuous bounding) for more elastic strength.

activities.name: Tuck Jump
activities.description: Vertical jump pulling knees to chest. Builds vertical power and core engagement.
activities.improvement_tip: Land soft, knees tracking over toes. If form breaks down (collapsing knees, slow turnover), end the set — fatigued plyos teach bad patterns.

activities.name: Medicine Ball Slam
activities.description: Lift medicine ball overhead, slam down with full body. Trains rotational and overhead power; great finisher.
activities.improvement_tip: Use a non-bouncing slam ball. Add rotational variants (over-shoulder slam) once base slam is comfortable at your target weight.

categories.name: Stretching
categories.description: Targeted mobility for areas commonly tightened by lifting, sitting, and squash. 5-15 min total per session is plenty.
categories.general_progression_tip: Range of motion improves slowly — track the same stretch in the same conditions weekly, not daily. 'Improvement' is a 1-10 self-rated depth or a measurable reach distance.

activities.name: Couch Stretch
activities.description: Rear foot elevated on couch/wall, front knee bent, hip extended. Targets hip flexors and quads — the #1 tight area for lifters and runners.

metrics.label: Depth rating (1-10)
metrics.note: 10 = upright torso with rear hip in full extension

activities.improvement_tip: Aim for 2 min per side, daily if possible. The first 30 seconds are tightness; the real change happens in the back half of the hold.

activities.name: 90/90 Hip Stretch
activities.description: Both hips at 90 degrees, one in front (internal rotation), one to the side (external rotation). Best single drill for hip rotational mobility.
activities.improvement_tip: Goal is to lower your chest to your front knee with both sit bones on the floor. Add an active version (lift-offs) once the static version feels easy.

activities.name: Pigeon Pose
activities.description: Front leg bent across body, back leg extended straight back, fold forward. Targets glutes and external hip rotators.
activities.improvement_tip: If your hip on the bent-leg side is way off the floor, prop it with a folded towel — fighting that gap creates compensation patterns elsewhere.

activities.name: Pancake Stretch
activities.description: Seated wide-legged forward fold. Targets adductors and hamstrings simultaneously.

metrics.label: Forehead-to-floor
metrics.note: Smaller distance = closer to fully flat

activities.improvement_tip: PNF protocol works well here: 30s passive hold, 10s gentle muscle contraction (push knees down), 30s deeper passive hold. Repeat 3x.

activities.name: Thoracic Rotation (Open Book)
activities.description: Side-lying or quadruped, rotate upper back and reach overhead. Targets thoracic spine — typically stiff in lifters.
activities.improvement_tip: Pause at end-range for 2 seconds each rep — that's where the change happens. Pair with deep exhale at end-range.

activities.name: Shoulder Dislocates (Band/PVC)
activities.description: Hold a band or PVC pipe, rotate it from in front to behind your back, keeping arms straight. Restores shoulder overhead mobility.
activities.improvement_tip: Start as wide as needed for pain-free rotation. Narrow the grip by 1 inch every 2 weeks until you reach shoulder-width.

activities.name: Calf Stretch (Wall)
activities.description: Foot against wall, lean forward — separate sets for gastrocnemius (straight knee) and soleus (bent knee). Critical for runners and squash players.

metrics.label: Knee-to-wall distance
metrics.note: Distance from toes to wall when knee touches = measure of dorsiflexion

activities.improvement_tip: Knee-to-wall test: stand facing a wall, push knee forward to touch wall. Distance from toes to wall is your dorsiflexion. Goal is 5+ inches.

categories.name: Recovery
categories.description: Active and passive recovery modalities. The work is done; this is what lets adaptation happen.
categories.general_progression_tip: Recovery isn't about doing more — it's about consistency. Pick 2-3 modalities you'll actually do and rotate them, rather than trying to stack everything every day.

activities.name: Foam Rolling
activities.description: Self-myofascial release on quads, glutes, IT band, lats, t-spine. Moderate pressure, not pain-inducing.
activities.improvement_tip: If a spot is genuinely tender, hold pressure for 30-45s — don't roll through it. Track pre/post tension over weeks; chronically tight areas should trend down.

activities.name: Mobility Flow
activities.description: Continuous sequence linking 5-8 mobility movements (e.g., World's Greatest Stretch, deep squat to pike, t-spine windmills). Low intensity, full body.

metrics.label: Perceived quality (1-10)
metrics.note: How fluid did the movements feel?

activities.improvement_tip: Build a fixed 10-minute sequence and run it daily. Same sequence weekly = visible improvement; constantly switching exercises = no benchmark.

activities.name: Easy Walk
activities.description: 20-60 minute walk at low intensity. Underrated — promotes blood flow without adding training stress.
activities.improvement_tip: Walks aren't a workout — don't push the pace. The benefit is parasympathetic recovery, which is destroyed by going hard.

activities.name: Sauna
activities.description: Dry heat exposure, typically 170-200°F. Heat acclimation has cardiovascular and recovery benefits.
activities.improvement_tip: 4 sessions x 20 min/week is the dose with the strongest research support. Hydrate before, during, and after — heat dehydrates fast.

activities.name: Cold Plunge / Cold Shower
activities.description: Cold water exposure, 50-60°F. Sympathetic nervous system activation; controversial for muscle adaptation but useful for recovery between same-day sessions.
activities.improvement_tip: AVOID immediately after lifting if hypertrophy is the goal — cold blunts the muscle-building signal for hours. Use it on rest days or after squash/cardio instead.

activities.name: Yoga / Restorative Flow
activities.description: Restorative or yin-style yoga, 20-45 minutes. Combines mobility, breathing, and parasympathetic activation.
activities.improvement_tip: Restorative or yin styles serve recovery best. Avoid power/vinyasa flows on heavy lifting days — they add training stress instead of dissipating it.

activities.name: Box Breathing
activities.description: Inhale 4s, hold 4s, exhale 4s, hold 4s — 5-10 minute sessions. Activates parasympathetic recovery; useful pre-sleep.
activities.improvement_tip: Track HR drop from start to end of session. A 10+ bpm drop in 5 minutes signals strong parasympathetic activation. If the drop shrinks over weeks, you may be over-stressed.

activities.name: Sleep
activities.description: Not an activity per se — but the single most important recovery input. Track it.
activities.improvement_tip: Consistency of bed/wake times matters more than total hours. A consistent 7.5 hours beats a chaotic 8.5. Track for 4 weeks before drawing conclusions.
